allopack.title: The Water Cycle — Earth's Recycling System
allopack.author: AlloFlow flagship (authored by Claude, reviewed by A. Pomeranz)
allopack.standards: NGSS MS-ESS2-4 (water cycling through Earth systems driven by sunlight and gravity); CCSS.ELA-LITERACY.RST.6-8.4 (domain vocabulary)

data.body:
**Due:** (your teacher will tell you)

Water has been recycling itself on Earth for about four billion years — the water in your bottle once floated in a cloud, and maybe once sat in a glacier. Tonight you become a water-cycle expert.

1. Read **Journey of a Raindrop** first. Read it out loud if you can — or use the Read Aloud button.
2. Open the **Glossary** and play the **Crossword** and the **Word Scramble** until you win each one.
3. Keep **The Four Moves of Water** chart open while you work — it is your cheat sheet, and using it is allowed.
4. Try the **Sort It: Where Is the Water Going?** activity — drag each card to the right stage.
5. Take the **Check Your Understanding** quiz. It is not a grade — wrong answers just show us what to practice.
6. Use the **Sentence Frames** to write two sentences about the water cycle in your own words.

When every goal below has a checkmark, you are done — and you have earned it. 🌧️➡️☀️

objectives.label: I taught someone at home one new word

history.title: Journey of a Raindrop
history.data:
## Journey of a Raindrop

Right now, the Sun is lifting water into the sky — and it does not even touch it. Sunlight warms oceans, lakes, and puddles until some of the water changes from a liquid into an invisible gas called **water vapor**. This change is called **evaporation**. You have seen its results every time a puddle "disappears" on a sunny sidewalk. The puddle did not vanish. It moved.

Plants join in, too. Water travels up from the roots and escapes through tiny holes in the leaves — a process called **transpiration**. A single large tree can send hundreds of liters of water into the air on a hot day, like a slow, silent fountain.

High in the sky, the air is much colder. Cold air cannot hold as much water vapor, so the vapor changes back into liquid — billions of droplets so small they float. This is **condensation**, and a cloud is exactly that: a crowd of floating droplets. A cloud is not made of smoke or cotton. It is water you can fly through.

Inside a cloud, droplets bump into each other and merge, growing heavier and heavier. When they become too heavy for the air to hold, gravity wins. Water falls as rain, snow, sleet, or hail — all of these are **precipitation**. Whether you get rain or snow depends mostly on how cold the air is on the way down.

Where does the fallen water go? Some of it flows downhill across the surface as **runoff**, joining streams and rivers that carry it back toward the ocean. Some soaks into the ground and becomes **groundwater**, filling the spaces between soil and rock like water filling a sponge. Lakes, rivers, oceans, and underground layers store the water in **collection** — until the Sun lifts it again.

Here is the amazing part: Earth almost never gets new water. The same water has been evaporating, condensing, falling, and collecting for about **four billion years**. The water in your water bottle may once have been in a dinosaur's pond, a Roman fountain, or a cloud over the ocean. The water cycle is Earth's original recycling system — powered by the Sun, pulled by gravity, and it never stops.

data.def: The change of liquid water into water vapor (a gas), usually caused by heat from the Sun.
The change of water vapor back into liquid droplets — this is how clouds form.
Water falling from clouds as rain, snow, sleet, or hail.
Water gathering in oceans, lakes, rivers, and underground after it falls.
Water leaving a plant through tiny holes in its leaves and entering the air as vapor.
Water that flows over the land surface into streams and rivers instead of soaking in.
Water stored underground in the spaces between soil and rock.
Water in its gas form — invisible and mixed into the air.
The force that pulls precipitation down to Earth and pulls rivers toward the ocean.
A set of events that repeats in the same order, over and over, with no end.

sections.label: UP
bullets: Evaporation — the Sun lifts water as invisible vapor
Transpiration — plants release water from their leaves
sections.iconPrompt: an upward arrow rising from a puddle toward the sun

sections.label: TOGETHER
bullets: Condensation — cold air turns vapor back into droplets
A cloud IS billions of floating droplets
sections.iconPrompt: tiny droplets gathering into a cloud

sections.label: DOWN
bullets: Precipitation — rain, snow, sleet, or hail
Gravity wins when droplets grow too heavy

sections.label: STORED
bullets: Runoff — flows over land into rivers
Groundwater — soaks between soil and rock
Collection — oceans, lakes, and ice hold it
sections.iconPrompt: a lake with an underground layer beneath it

sections.label: REMEMBER
bullets: Earth almost never gets new water
Powered by the Sun, pulled by gravity
The cycle has no start and no end

history.title: Sort It: Where Is the Water Going?

items.content: A puddle shrinks on a sunny sidewalk
A tree releases water through its leaves
Your cold drink glass gets wet on the outside
Tiny floating droplets crowd together in the sky
Hail bounces off a car roof
Rainwater soaks deep between soil and rock
A river carries water toward the ocean
Steam rises from a hot pot of soup
Fog forms over a cold field in the morning

questions.question: A puddle disappears on a sunny day. What actually happened to the water?
options: It changed into water vapor and mixed into the air
It soaked completely into the concrete
The Sun destroyed the water
It turned into oxygen
questions.correctAnswer: It changed into water vapor and mixed into the air

questions.question: What is a cloud made of?
options: Billions of tiny floating water droplets
Smoke collected from the ground
Water vapor only, with no liquid at all
Cotton-like fibers of frozen air
questions.correctAnswer: Billions of tiny floating water droplets

questions.question: Why does condensation happen high in the sky?
options: Cold air cannot hold as much water vapor as warm air
The sky pushes water droplets together
There is no gravity high in the sky
Clouds attract water from the ocean
questions.correctAnswer: Cold air cannot hold as much water vapor as warm air

questions.question: Which of these is NOT a form of precipitation?
options: Fog sitting over a field
Snow falling on a mountain
Hail bouncing off a roof
Sleet hitting a window

questions.question: About how old is the water you drank today?
options: About four billion years — Earth keeps recycling the same water
Brand new — rain makes fresh water from nothing
About one hundred years old
As old as the pipes it traveled through
questions.correctAnswer: About four billion years — Earth keeps recycling the same water

questions.question: Explain the difference between runoff and groundwater. Where does each kind of water go?
questions.expectedAnswer: Runoff flows over the surface of the land into streams and rivers (and toward the ocean). Groundwater soaks into the ground and is stored in spaces between soil and rock.

questions.question: The water cycle is powered by two things: one lifts water up, one pulls it down. Name both and say what each one does.
questions.expectedAnswer: The Sun powers evaporation by warming water so it rises as vapor; gravity pulls precipitation down to Earth and pulls rivers toward the ocean.

reflections.text: What surprised you most about where your drinking water has been?
Describe one place in your neighborhood where you could SEE part of the water cycle happening this week.

items.text: When the Sun warms water, it changes into ____ through a process called ____.
Clouds form when water vapor ____ because the air high up is ____.
Precipitation can fall as ____, ____, or ____, depending on ____.
After rain falls, some water becomes runoff, which means it ____, and some becomes groundwater, which means it ____.
The water cycle never makes new water; instead, it ____ the same water because ____.
One place I saw the water cycle in real life was ____, where I noticed ____.

data.rubric:
| Criteria | 1 — Starting | 3 — Getting There | 5 — Scientist |
|---|---|---|---|
| Vocabulary | Uses everyday words only | Uses 1-2 glossary words correctly | Uses 3+ glossary words correctly and naturally |
| Accuracy | Ideas partly mixed up | Mostly correct with one slip | Every statement scientifically correct |
| Completeness | Fills some blanks | Fills all blanks simply | Fills all blanks with detail and connects ideas |

history.title: But Wait — Water Questions Kids Actually Ask

data.question: Is it true I might be drinking dinosaur water? 🦕
data.answer: Pretty much! Earth has been recycling the same water for about four billion years, so the molecules in your bottle have been through countless clouds, rivers, and yes — probably a dinosaur or two.

data.question: Why doesn't the ocean overflow if rivers keep pouring into it?
data.answer: Because the Sun is constantly lifting water OUT of the ocean by evaporation — roughly as fast as rivers pour it in. The cycle keeps it balanced.

data.question: Why is rain fresh water when it evaporates from the salty ocean? 🧂
data.answer: Great catch — when water evaporates, the salt gets left behind. Only the water molecules rise as vapor, so clouds (and rain) are salt-free.

data.question: Can the water cycle ever just stop?
data.answer: Not while the Sun shines and gravity pulls. It would take losing one of those to stop it — and neither is going anywhere for billions of years.

data.question: Where does my breath fog go on a cold day?
data.answer: That IS the water cycle, in miniature! Warm water vapor from your lungs hits cold air and condenses into tiny droplets — a little personal cloud — then evaporates again into the air.

data.question: Is snow just frozen rain?
data.answer: Not exactly. Snow forms when water vapor turns straight into ice crystals inside a very cold cloud. Sleet is closer to frozen rain — raindrops that freeze on the way down.
